clients.name: Sunway Group

scenarioImpacts.base: Brent ~$102.50 est. inflates construction fuel and cement-kiln energy costs ~55% vs Q4 2025. PM Anwar confirmed Petronas is now a net fuel importer (Apr 4) — MYR no longer benefits from exporter premium; USD/MYR ~4.08 est. USD-denominated material imports more expensive. Property development pipeline faces margin compression; infrastructure segment partially insulated by fixed-price government contracts. Malaysia June supply uncertainty adds domestic energy cost risk.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Brent toward $115/bbl raises Sunway's construction input cost base by est. 35-40%. Cement and steel surcharges compound margin pressure. MYR toward 4.15+ vs USD (no longer oil-exporter-supported) increases unhedged USD material exposure materially; revolving credit facility drawings likely to increase.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Brent at $150+ triggers cement and fuel surcharges across all project sites; est. MYR 420-500M incremental cost on in-flight portfolio. Malaysia energy emergency deepens — June supply gap risk; property demand destruction from energy inflation; presales slowdown accelerates refinancing pressure on development-linked SPV bonds.

talkingPoints: Brent ~$102.50 est. (+57% vs Q4 2025) — Sunway's construction fuel and diesel procurement must be fully hedged for H2 2026 immediately; PM Anwar's Apr 4 Petronas net-importer disclosure means MYR no longer benefits from oil exporter premium; unhedged exposure represents est. MYR 55-80M annualised risk per $10/bbl move.

Malaysia energy emergency (Petronas net importer; June supply uncertain; RON95 quota cut; WFH mandate) signals structural domestic cost pressure — front-load any construction material imports from non-Gulf sources now before June supply squeeze tightens freight and input pricing further.

Property development SPV bonds maturing 2027-2028 should be pre-financed immediately — iTraxx Asia IG at ~198bp (+8bp today) and ASEAN HY at ~575bp are re-widening on IRGC ship seizures; do not assume ceasefire extension normalises the credit window; execute term-out of SPV debt before spreads widen further.

clients.name: IJM Corporation

scenarioImpacts.base: Plantation segment benefits from CPO prices tracking Brent ~$102.50 est. (CPO ~4,980 MYR/mt, +1.0%); infrastructure segment faces significantly higher diesel and bitumen costs. Shipping costs for palm oil exports elevated — IRGC ship seizures (Apr 22) and Cape rerouting persist. USD/MYR ~4.08 est.; PM Anwar confirmed Petronas net fuel importer (Apr 4) — MYR no longer benefits from exporter premium.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Brent toward $115/bbl translates to CPO prices tracking 5,200+ MYR/mt — plantation revenue improves but is offset by infrastructure cost inflation. Port and logistics operations face surging bunker costs; IRGC ship seizures (MSC Francesca, Epaminondas) threaten Singapore Strait/Malacca throughput. Net: marginally negative at group level given infrastructure-heavy earnings mix.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Extended Hormuz closure redirects ASEAN-Middle East shipping; IJM's port concessions face volume decline. Malaysia June supply gap deepens — plantation logistics disrupted as Malacca rerouting congestion adds transit costs. Refinancing of MYR 1.2bn infrastructure sukuk requires urgent review as iTraxx now ~198bp and rising toward 230bp+ in tail.

talkingPoints: CPO prices ~4,980 MYR/mt (+1.0%), tracking Brent ~$102.50 on IRGC ship seizures — IJM plantation segment earnings uplift is real, but Petronas net-importer disclosure (Anwar Apr 4) means Malaysia no longer benefits from oil exporter premium; USD/MYR pressure at ~4.08 erodes USD-export repatriation value.

Infrastructure contract pipeline: all active tenders must include fuel escalation provisions — Brent at ~$102.50 makes any fixed-price fuel clause in a 2026 tender extremely risky; IRGC ship seizures raise tail risk to 40%, extending high-oil environment through 2026.

MYR sukuk maturing 2026-2027: refinance immediately — iTraxx Asia IG at ~198bp (+8bp today) and ASEAN HY at ~575bp (+15bp) are re-widening on IRGC seizures; do not rely on Malaysian exporter-premium spread compression; window is closing.

clients.name: Wilmar International

scenarioImpacts.base: Palm oil/biodiesel pricing directly tracks Brent at $104.73 - CPO refining margins and biodiesel mandates (Indonesia B40, Malaysia B20) remain highly compelling. Bunker costs (~12% of Wilmar's cost base) reflect elevated freight environment. Dubai Airport attack adds logistical uncertainty for Gulf cargo legs. USD/SGD at 1.2782 broadly stable.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil at $120/bbl creates dual impact: biodiesel margin expansion (+USD 100-120/MT) partially offset by bunker cost surge. Wilmar's Middle East cargo legs face insurance void and port-access risk post-Dubai Airport attack. Supply chain re-routing around Hormuz adds 10-18 days. SGD at 1.28 broadly stable but demand for USD liquidity rises.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Hormuz and Gulf hub closure disrupts Wilmar's Middle East bulk trade; est. $250-320M incremental annual logistics cost at $150/bbl oil. Biodiesel revenue surge partially offsets. Revolving credit utilisation spikes; bank appetite for commodity trader credit under sanctions pressure tightens further.

talkingPoints: Brent at $121.40/bbl makes Indonesia's B40 biodiesel mandate exceptionally compelling - Wilmar's biodiesel refining economics are at their strongest level; model incremental EBITDA vs $60/bbl base case at $600-700M; however, JKM $23.40 (new Platts) spikes feedstock and bunker costs materially - net exposure is positive but narrowing.

Saudi airstrikes on IRGC at Bandar Abbas (Mar 20) and Iran's Abqaiq missile strike (Mar 22) - all Wilmar Middle East cargo legs face compounded insurance void and port-access risk; review every UAE/Gulf-port-dependent shipment schedule and confirm Cape-route contingency is fully operational; bunker hedging should extend to Q2 2027.

USD revolving credit facility: with iTraxx Asia IG at ~185bp and ASEAN HY at ~555bp, execute RCF drawdown now; Saudi-Iran direct exchange eliminates near-term normalisation and will further tighten commodity trade finance bank appetite under escalating secondary sanctions pressure.

clients.name: Hyflux

scenarioImpacts.base: Desalination operations are extremely energy-intensive - electricity costs represent 35-45% of total operating cost. Brent at $104.73/bbl drives Singapore grid power costs significantly higher vs Q4 2025. Legacy restructuring means Hyflux balance sheet remains highly leveraged; restructuring creditor repayment schedule linked to operational cashflows now under severe energy cost pressure.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Power cost at $120/bbl oil equivalent pushes desalination OPEX above contractual tariff recovery thresholds. Refinancing creditors become anxious; restructuring plan covenant compliance at risk. ASEAN HY spreads at ~510bp make any new capital raise prohibitively expensive - further restructuring negotiation is required.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Energy cost spiral under $150+ oil renders Hyflux's desalination economics non-viable without emergency tariff relief. Creditor acceleration risk materialises; Singapore government may need to intervene to ensure water supply continuity. Restructuring collapses into administration unless sovereign backstop is committed.

talkingPoints: Hyflux desalination OPEX is 38-42% electricity; with Singapore power prices tracking Brent at $121.40 and JKM $23.40, the cost-recovery gap vs contracted tariff has widened to est. SGD 55-70M annually - urgent need to activate emergency renegotiation of power purchase agreements and escalation clauses in government supply contracts.

Restructuring plan repayment milestones fall due H2 2026 - ASEAN HY spreads at ~555bp fully close the capital markets window; engage private-credit, DFI/multilateral bridge financing, and quasi-sovereign structures now; the Saudi-Iran direct exchange removes any remaining market normalisation timeline for 2026.

Base scenario probability is 8%; tail scenario is 40% - Saudi airstrikes on IRGC (Mar 20) and Iran's Abqaiq missile strike (Mar 22) eliminate any near-term de-escalation pathway; capital access is structurally constrained through H2 2026; pursue restructuring resolution with maximum urgency before creditor confidence deteriorates further.

clients.name: Sapura Energy

scenarioImpacts.base: Brent at $104.73/bbl is well above the $65-70 E&P capex trigger threshold for most upstream clients; new contract awards improving but execution lagging 2-3 quarters. Sapura's high leverage (net debt ~MYR 10bn) means widening ASEAN HY spreads (~510bp) directly impact refinancing cost. Shipping utilisation for marine vessels recovering modestly.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil at $120/bbl boosts E&P client confidence but capex cycle lag means Sapura revenue uplift is 6-9 months behind oil price. Existing debt refinancing under severe stress: ASEAN HY at 510bp+ makes bond issuance economically unfeasible. Dubai Airport attack adds Gulf project logistics risk for any Sapura upstream contracts in the region.
scenarioImpacts.tail: E&P capex freezes under $150 oil uncertainty (client hedging collapse) - paradoxically negative for oil services despite high prices. Sapura debt covenant breach risk materialises; government-linked creditor (Petronas, Maybank) intervention required. Potential restructuring or asset sale of marine fleet to service debt obligations.

talkingPoints: Brent at $121.40/bbl is well above Sapura's E&P client capex threshold - but order book recovery lags oil price by 2-3 quarters; model 2026 revenue conservatively at $95/bbl equivalent; the Saudi-Iran direct exchange on Mar 20-22 adds execution uncertainty to any Gulf-region upstream contracts.

Sapura's MYR debt maturities 2026-2027: iTraxx Asia IG at ~185bp and ASEAN HY at ~555bp - the unsecured window is closed; pivot immediately to secured bank refinancing, bilateral lender negotiations, and DFI-backed structures before covenants are at risk; the Saudi-Iran exchange extends the risk premium indefinitely.

Saudi airstrikes on Bandar Abbas (Mar 20) and Iran's Abqaiq missile strike (Mar 22) require immediate review of all Sapura Gulf-region project insurance (P&I cover void), force majeure provisions, and OFAC/EU sanctions compliance - assume any Gulf project insurance is effectively void until further notice.

clients.name: PTTEP

scenarioImpacts.base: PTTEP is a net beneficiary of Brent at $104.73/bbl - upstream producer with production cost est. $28-32/bbl; operating margin expands materially. USD/THB at 32.47 (weakened 2.2% from Mar 13) reduces USD revenue on repatriation vs earlier this week. LNG and Thailand domestic gas supply obligations provide stable domestic revenue floor.
scenarioImpacts.stress: Oil at $120/bbl boosts PTTEP upstream EBITDA by est. USD 600-750M annually. However, Dubai Airport attack and Gulf hub targeting create sanctions compliance risk for any PTTEP Middle East JV operations. THB weakening toward 34-35 in a full stress scenario reduces repatriation value materially.
scenarioImpacts.tail: Extended Hormuz and Gulf hub closure raises PTTEP's shipping and logistics cost for LNG offtake; rerouting adds significant cost to Mozambique and Myanmar offshore logistics. THB depreciation toward 36-38 in a systemic tail scenario would reduce USD revenue translation. Sanctions scrutiny on Middle East upstream JVs warrants immediate compliance review.

talkingPoints: PTTEP is directly benefiting from Brent at $121.40 - upstream production cost at $28-32/bbl means current oil price generates operating margins of $89-93/bbl; accelerate hedging of remaining 2026 unhedged production at current levels before any ceasefire retracement; do not wait for the Saudi-Iran exchange to de-escalate.

THB est. 33.80 (Mar 22) as JKM $23.40 new Platts assessment spikes Thailand's gas import costs - BOT easing is now effectively impossible; Thailand 10Y est. 2.18%; execute H2 2026 prefunding immediately while the local-duration window is still technically open; every week of delay risks a wider spread.

Saudi airstrikes on IRGC at Bandar Abbas (Mar 20) and Iran's Abqaiq missile strike (Mar 22) intensify secondary sanctions compliance risk on PTTEP's Middle East JV exposure - conduct an immediate jurisdictional review of all Gulf-region exploration assets and JV partner relationships for full OFAC and EU compliance.
